Nonetheless, it appears to be the sixth or seventh longest extant eagle species.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /> The [[wingspan]] of martial eagles can range from {{convert|188|to|240|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}}.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="HBW" /><ref>[http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/site/accounts/information/Polemaetus%20bellicosus.html Polemaetus bellicosus- University of Michigan Species Profile]</ref><ref>Sinclair, I., Hockey, P., Tarboton, W., Ryan, P., & Perrins, N. (2020). ''Sasol birds of southern Africa''. Penguin Random House South Africa.</ref> Wingspans of as much as {{convert|260|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}} have been reported but may be unsubstantiated.<ref>Grambo, R. L. (1999). ''Eagles''. Voyageur Press.</ref><ref>van Rooyen, C. (2008). ''Bird impact assessment study''.</ref> Average wingspans have been claimed of {{convert|205|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}} and {{convert|207.5|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}} for the species, however ten measured martial eagles in the wild were found to average {{convert|211.9|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}} in wingspan. Thus, the martial eagle appears to average fourth in wingspan among living eagles, behind only the [[Steller's sea eagle]] (''Haliaeetus pelagicus''), the [[white-tailed eagle]] (''Haliaeetus albicilla'') and the [[wedge-tailed eagle]] (''Aquila audax''), in roughly that order.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Redman" /><ref name="Mendelsohn">Mendelsohn, J.M., Kemp, A.C., Biggs, H.C., Biggs, R., & Brown, C.J. (1989). ''Wing areas, wing loadings and wing spans of 66 species of African raptors''. Ostrich, 60(1), 35-42.</ref><ref>Saito, K. (2009). ''Lead poisoning of Steller's Sea-Eagle (Haliaeetus pelagicus) and White-tailed Eagle (Haliaeetus albicilla) caused by the ingestion of lead bullets and slugs''. Hokkaido Japan. In RT Watson, M. Fuller, M. Pokras, and WG Hunt (Eds.). Ingestion of Lead from Spent Ammunition: Implications for Wildlife and Humans. The Peregrine Fund, Boise, Idaho, USA.</ref><ref name="Wood">{{cite book | author = Wood, Gerald |url=https://archive.org/details/guinnessbookofan00wood | title = The Guinness Book of Animal Facts and Feats |year=1983 | publisher = Guinness Superlatives | isbn = 978-0-85112-235-9 | url-access = registration}}</ref> For a species that is fairly homogeneous in its genetic make-up, the body mass of martial eagles is surprisingly variable. To some extent, the variation of body masses in the species is attributable to considerable reverse sexual dimorphism as well as varying environmental conditions of various eagle populations.<ref name="Biggs">Biggs, H.C., Kemp, A.C., Mendelsohn, H.P. & Mendelsohn, J.M. (1979). ''Weights of South African Raptors and Owls''. Durban Museum Novitates, 12: 73-81.</ref> Unsexed martial eagles from various studies have been found to have weighed an average of {{convert|3.93|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 17 birds, {{convert|3.97|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 20 birds and {{convert|4.23|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in 20 birds while the average weight of martial eagles shot by game wardens in the early 20th century in [[South Africa]] was listed as {{convert|4.71|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Mendelsohn" /><ref name="Biggs" /><ref>Bright, J.A., Marugán-Lobón, J., Cobb, S.N., & Rayfield, E.J. (2016). ''The shapes of bird beaks are highly controlled by nondietary factors''. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, 113(19), 5352-5357.</ref><ref name="CRC">{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=1992 |isbn=978-0-8493-4258-5}}</ref><ref name="Stevenson-Hamilton">Stevenson-Hamilton, J. (1954). ''Wild life in South Africa''. Cassell and Co., London.</ref> In weight range, the martial eagle broadly overlaps in size with the [[golden eagle]] (''Aquila chrysaetos'') and [[Verreaux's eagle]] (and is even exceeded by them in maximum known body mass). Based on numerous studies, the martial eagle appears to be mildly heavier than the Verreaux's eagle but (derived from the globally combined body mass of its various races), the mean body masses of golden and martial eagles are identical at approximately {{convert|4.17|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. This renders the golden and martial eagles as tied as the largest African eagles (by body mass but not in total length or wingspan, in which the martial bests the golden), as well as the heaviest two species of booted eagle in the world and as tied as the sixth heaviest eagles in the world, after the three largest species of [[sea eagle]] ([[Steller's sea eagle|Steller's]] being the heaviest extant, the others ranking 4th and 5th), the [[harpy eagle]] (''Harpia harpyja'') and the [[Philippine eagle]] (''Pithecophaga jefferyi'').<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Mendelsohn" /><ref name="Biggs" /><ref name="CRC" /><ref name="CRC2">{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}}</ref><ref name="Watson">{{cite book|author=Watson, Jeff |title=The Golden Eagle|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=aj5MNvCkun0C|year=2010|publisher=A&C Black|isbn=978-1-4081-1420-9}}</ref> The longest African eagle (and second longest booted eagle after the [[wedge-tailed eagle]] (''Aquila audax'')) is the [[crowned eagle]] by virtue of its relatively longer tail, as its body weight is slightly less than these three heaviest booted eagle species.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="CRC" />

===Sexual dimorphism===
[[File:Martial Eagle Looking for Guineafowl.jpg|thumb|Martial Eagle near [[Okaukuejo]] in [[Etosha National Park]].]]
Martial eagles are highly sexually dimorphic. While females average about 10% larger in linear dimension, in body mass, the sexual dimorphism of martial eagles is more pronounced. Males reportedly can weigh from {{convert|2.2|to|3.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. Seven males in southern Africa averaged {{convert|3.17|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and five in another dataset averaged {{convert|3.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="CRC2" /><ref name="Musindo">Musindo, P.T. (2012). ''Morphological variation in bills and claws in relation to Prey type in Southern African Birds of Prey (Orders Falconiformes and Strigiformes)''. Thesis, University of Zimbabwe.</ref><ref name="vanEeden">van Eeden, R., Whitfield, D.P., Botha, A., & Amar, A. (2017). ''Ranging behaviour and habitat preferences of the Martial Eagle: Implications for the conservation of a declining apex predator''. PLoS ONE, 12(3), e0173956.</ref> Twelve adult males in [[Maasai Mara]], [[Kenya]] averaged {{convert|3.45|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Hatfield">Hatfield, R. S. (2018). ''Diet and Space Use of the Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus) in the Maasai Mara Region of Kenya''. Theses and Dissertations--Forestry and Natural Resources. 44.</ref> Meanwhile, females can weigh from {{convert|4.45|to|6.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. In southern Africa, seven females averaging {{convert|4.95|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="CRC2" /> Elsewhere, a claim was made of an average of {{convert|5.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}} almost certainly describes a sample entirely of female specimens.<ref>''L. Brent Vaughan Hill's Practical Reference Library Volume II'' (New York, NY: Dixon, Hanson and Company, 1906)</ref> In the Maasai Mara, 7 females averaged {{convert|4.67|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Hatfield" /> Reports of males weighing as much as {{convert|5.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and females weighing as little as {{convert|3.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}} are known but may possibly represent individual eagles misidentified by sex, which is reportedly not infrequent due to mistakes in the field.<ref name="vanEeden" /><ref>Kovařík, J. (2002). ''1809-Orel proti orlu''. Hart.</ref><ref name="Smeenk">Smeenk, C. (1974). ''Comparative-ecological studies of some East African birds of prey''. Ardea 62 (1-2) : 1-97.</ref> Thus the dimorphism by weight is roughly 36% in favor of the female, which is unusually out of sync with the linear differences between the sexes. For example, the [[greater spotted eagle]] (''Clanga clanga''), the most sexually dimorphic booted eagle overall with a linear difference between the sexes of 20%, has around the same level of sexual dimorphism by body mass as the martial eagle which show about half as much linear dimorphism.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Musindo" /> Besides being larger, females tend to have more heavy spotting on the underside than males but this may be not a reliable field mark.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /> In standard measurements, male martial eagles measure {{convert|560|to|610|mm|in|abbr=on}} in [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] size, {{convert|273|to|280|mm|in|abbr=on}} in [[tail]] length and {{convert|97|to|118|mm|in|abbr=on}} in tarsus length. Meanwhile, females measure {{convert|605|to|675|mm|in|abbr=on}} in wing chord, {{convert|280|to|320|mm|in|abbr=on}} in tail length and {{convert|114|to|130|mm|in|abbr=on}} tarsal length.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /> Overall, the bulk and much more massive proportions of females, which include more robust feet and longer tarsi, may at times allow experienced observers to sex lone birds in the wild.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="vanEeden" />

===Colouring and field identification===
[[File:Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus) juvenile (17142198408).jpg|thumb|right|A typically pale immature martial eagle in [[Kruger National Park]].]]
The adult's [[plumage]] consists of dark brown coloration on the upperparts, head and upper chest, with an occasional slightly lighter edging to these feathers. The dark feathers can appear grayish, blackish or even plum-colored depending on lighting conditions. The body underparts are feathered white with sparse but conspicuous blackish-brown spotting. The underwing coverts are dark brown, with the remiges being pale streaked with black, overall imparting the wings of adults a dark look. The underside of the tail has similar barring as the remiges while the upperside is the same uniform brown as the back and upperwing coverts. The eyes of mature martial eagles are rich yellow, while the [[Beak|cere]] and large feet pale greenish and the talons black.  Martial eagles have a short erectile crest, which is typically neither prominent nor flared (unlike that of the [[crowned eagle]]) and generally appears as an angular back to a seemingly flat head. This species often perches in a quite upright position, with its long wings completely covering the tail, causing it be described as "standing" rather than "sitting" on a branch when perched. In flight, martial eagles bear long broad wings with relatively narrow rounded tips that can appear pointed at times depending on how the eagle is holding its wings. It is capable of flexible beats with gliding on flattish wings, or slightly raised in a [[Dihedral (aircraft)|dihedral]]. This species often spends a large portion of the day on the wing, more so than probably any other African eagles, and often at a great height.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="HBW" /> Juvenile martial eagles are conspicuously distinct in plumage with a pearly gray colour above with considerable white edging, as well as a speckled grey effect on crown and hind neck. The entire underside is conspicuously white. The wing coverts of juveniles are mottled grey-brown and white, with patterns of bars on primaries and tail that are similar to adult but lighter and greyer. In the fourth or fifth years, a very gradual increase to brownish feather speckling is noted but the back and crown remain a fairly pale grey. At this age, there may be increasing spots on throat and chest which coalesce into a gorget and some spots on abdomen may variably manifest as well.  The eyes of juveniles are dark brown. This species reaches adult plumage by its seventh year with the transition to adult plumage happening quite rapidly after many years in a little-changing juvenile plumage.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref>North, M.E.W. (1939). ''Field Notes on certain Raptorials and Water‐Birds in Kenya Colony—Part II''. Ibis, 3(4), 617-643.</ref>
[[File:Martial Eagle Front 2019-07-24.jpg|thumb|left|A perched adult martial eagle.]]
There are a few serious identification challenges for the species. The [[black-chested snake eagle]] (''Circaetus pectoralis'') is similar in overall colouring (despite its name it is brown on the chest and the back, being no darker than the adult martial eagle) to martial eagles but is markedly smaller, with a more prominent, rounded head with large eyes, plain, spotless abdomen, bare and whitish legs. In flight, the profile of the snake eagle is quite different, with nearly white (rather than dark brown) flight feathers and much smaller, narrower wings and a relatively larger tail. For juveniles, the main source for potential confusion is the juvenile [[crowned eagle]], which also regularly perches in an erect position. The proportions of crowned eagles are quite distinct from martial eagles as they have much shorter wings and a distinctly longer tail. The juvenile crowned eagle has a whiter head, more scaled back, and spotted thighs and legs lacking in the martial eagle. Beyond their distinct flight profile by wing and tail proportions, crowned eagles have whiter and more obviously banded flight-feathers and tail. Other large immature eagles in Africa tend to be much darker and more heavily marked both above and below than martial eagles.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" />

===Predatory physiology===
[[File:Martial eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus) juvenile (13817104204).jpg|thumb|left|Even immature martial eagles have formidable talons and feet.]]
Martial eagles have been noted as remarkable for their extremely keen eyesight (3.0–3.6 times human acuity), partly due to their eye being nearly as large as a human's eye. Due to this power, they can spot potential prey from a great distance, having been known to be able to spot prey from as far as {{convert|5|to|6|km|mi|abbr=on}} away.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref>{{cite journal |last=Shlaer |first=Robert |year=1972|title=An Eagle's Eye: Quality of the Retinal Image |journal=Science |volume=176 |issue=4037|pages=920–922 |doi= 10.1126/science.176.4037.920 |url= http://www.csee.wvu.edu/~xinl/library/papers/biology/eagle_eyes.pdf |access-date= 16 April 2012 |pmid=5033635 |bibcode=1972Sci...176..920S |s2cid=8034443 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20121130130226/http://www.csee.wvu.edu/~xinl/library/papers/biology/eagle_eyes.pdf |archive-date=30 November 2012}}</ref> Their visual acuity may rival some eagles from the genus ''[[Aquila (genus)|Aquila]]'' and some of the larger falcons as the greatest of all diurnal raptors.<ref>Reymond, L. (1985). ''Spatial visual acuity of the eagle Aquila audax: a behavioural, optical and anatomical investigation''. Vision research, 25(10), 1477-1491.</ref><ref>Fox, R., Lehmkuhle, S.W., & Westendorf, D.H. (1976). ''Falcon visual acuity''. Science, 192(4236), 263-265.</ref> The talons of martial eagles are impressive and can approach the size, especially in mature females, of those of the crowned eagle despite their slenderer metatarsus and toes compared to the crowned species.<ref>Chapin, J.P., & Lang, H. (1953). ''The birds of the Belgian Congo''. Part 3/by James P. Chapin. Bulletin of the AMNH; v. 75A.</ref> Accipitrids usually kill their prey with an elongated, sharp hind toe claw, which is referred to as the hallux claw and is reliably the largest talon in members of the accipitrid family.<ref>Fowler, D.W., Freedman, E.A., & Scannella, J.B. (2009). ''Predatory functional morphology in raptors: interdigital variation in talon size is related to prey restraint and immobilisation technique''. PLoS ONE, 4(11), e7999.</ref> The average length of the hallux claw in unsexed martial eagles from [[Tsavo East National Park]], [[Kenya]] was found to be {{convert|51.1|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Smeenk" /> In comparison, the average hallux claw of a large sample of golden eagles was similar at {{convert|51.7|mm|in|abbr=on}}. Meanwhile, the three largest clawed modern eagles were found to measure as such: in small samples, the [[Philippine eagle]] and crowned eagle had an average hallux claw length of {{convert|55.7|mm|in|abbr=on}} and {{convert|55.8|mm|in|abbr=on}}, respectively, and [[harpy eagle]]s have an average hallux claw length of approximately {{convert|63.3|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Tabaranza">{{cite web |url= http://haringibon2011.wordpress.com/2008/03/09/haribon-haring-ibon-king-bird/ |title=Haribon – ''Ha ring mga Ibon'', King of Birds |author= Blas R. Tabaranza Jr. |publisher=Haring Ibon's Flight… |access-date=2013-06-24|date=2008-03-09}}</ref><ref>Prout-Jones, D.V., & Kemp, A.G. (1997). ''Moult, plumage sequence and maintenance behaviour of a captive male and female crowned eagle, Stephanoaetus coronatus (Aves: Accipitridae)''. Annals of the Transvaal Museum, 36(Part 19).</ref><ref name="Bortolotti">{{cite journal |author=Bortolotti G.R. |year=1984 |title=Age and sex size variation in Golden Eagles |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |volume=55 |pages=54–66}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |title=Notes on the Harpy Eagle in British Guiana |journal=The Auk |volume=81 |issue=3 |pages=257–273 |author1=Fowler, J.M. |author2=Cope, J.B. |jstor=4082683 |year=1964 |doi=10.2307/4082683}}</ref> A further sample of martial eagles averaged {{convert|47.4|mm|in|abbr=on}} in 6 males and {{convert|56.2|mm|in|abbr=on}} in 7 females, with the median between the sexes being {{convert|52.3|mm|in|abbr=on}}, these being the largest talons of all booted eagles behind only the crowned eagle.<ref>Parry, S. J. (2001). ''The booted eagles (Aves: Accipitridae): perspectives in evolutionary biology''. University of London, University College London (United Kingdom).</ref> The inner claw on the front of the foot of the martial eagle is especially sizeable proportional to other extremities and unusually can approach, if not reach, the same size as the hallux claw.  This inner claw was found to average {{convert|46.1|mm|in|abbr=on}}, in comparison to that of the crowned eagle which measures {{convert|47.4|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Smeenk" /><ref>Worthy, T.H. & Holdaway, R.N. (2002). ''The Lost World of the Moa: Prehistoric Life of New Zealand''. Indiana University Press, {{ISBN|0253340349}}.</ref> The [[Tibiotarsus|tarsus]] is quite long in martial eagles, the fourth longest of any living eagle and the longest of any booted eagle species, seemingly an adaptation to prey capture in long grass, including potentially dangerous prey.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Lerner" /> The [[Beak|bill]] is of medium size relative to that of other large eagles, with a mean [[Beak|culmen]] length from Tsavo East of {{convert|43.7|mm|in|abbr=on}}. The bill is comparable to average bill length seen in golden eagles but is smaller than that of some other large members of the genus ''[[Aquila (genus)|Aquila]]''. Both harpy and crowned eagles have somewhat longer bills than a martial eagle, but that of the martial is particularly smaller than those of the large species of [[sea eagle]] (the Steller's sea eagle likely has the largest bill of all living eagles) and the [[Philippine eagle]].<ref name="Smeenk" /><ref name="Musindo" /><ref name="Bortolotti" /><ref name="Tabaranza" /><ref>Shephard, J.M., [[Carla P. Catterall|Catterall, C.P.]], & Hughes, J.M. (2004). ''Discrimination of sex in the White-bellied Sea-Eagle, ''Haliaeetus leucogaster'', using genetic and morphometric techniques''. Emu, 104(1), 83-87.</ref> The [[Beak|gape]] size of martial eagles is relatively large, however, being proportionally larger than in other booted eagle species behind (albeit considerably behind) the [[Indian spotted eagle]] (''Clanga hastata'') and the [[steppe eagle]] (''Aquila nipalensis'') in relative gape size, indicating a relative specialization towards swallowing large prey whole.<ref>Parry, S.J., Clark, W.S., & Prakash, V. (2002). ''On the taxonomic status of the Indian Spotted Eagle Aquila hastata''. Ibis, 144(4), 665-675.</ref><ref>Clark, W. S. (1992). ''The taxonomy of Steppe and Tawny Eagles, with criteria for separation of museum specimens and live eagles''. Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club, 112, 150-157.</ref>

===Voice===
The martial eagle is a weak and infrequent vocaliser. Little vocal activity has been reported even during the breeding season. The recorded contact call between pair-members consists of the birds, usually when perched, letting out a low mellow whistle, ''{{not a typo|ko-wee-oh}}''. More or less the same vocalisation is known to have been uttered by females when male brings food and repeated mildly by large begging young. During territorial aerial display and sometimes when perched, adults may utter a loud, trilling ''{{not a typo|klee-klee-klooeee-klooeee-kulee}}''. The territorial call may be heard from some distance. Recent fledglings also at times make this call. A soft ''{{not a typo|quolp}}'' may be heard, made by pairs around their nest, perhaps being a mutual contact call.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Brown" /> In comparison, the [[crowned eagle]] is highly vocal especially in the context of breeding.<ref name="Brown" />

==Habitat==
[[File:Kampfadler03.jpg|thumb|right|Martial eagles tend to be attracted to thorny or tall trees in otherwise fairly open savanna.]]
The martial eagle is to some degree adaptable to varied [[habitat]]s but shows an overall preference for [[Woodland|open woods]] and [[woodland edge]]s, wooded [[savannah]] and [[Veld|thornbush]] habitats. The martial eagle has been recorded at elevations of up to {{convert|3000|m|ft|abbr=on}} but is not a true mountain dwelling species and resident eagles do not usually exceed an elevation of {{convert|1500|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /> These eagles also avoid [[Old-growth forest|closed-canopy forests]] and hyper-arid [[desert]].<ref name="Atlas" /> As such it is mostly absent from [[Guinean Forests of West Africa|Guinean]] and [[Congolian forests]], despite the species' requirement for large trees for nesting purposes. It is shown that martial eagles can inhabit forests locally in areas where openings occur.<ref name="Brown" /> For example, in a bird atlas for the country of [[Kenya]], perhaps surprisingly, 88% of martial eagles were found to reside in well-wooded areas and they occurred in areas where annual rainfall exceeded {{convert|250|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref>Lewis, A., & Pomeroy, D. (1989). ''A bird atlas of Kenya''. CRC Press.</ref> In southern Africa, they have adapted to seemingly more open habitats than elsewhere in their range, such as [[semi-desert]] and open savanna with scattered trees, [[Hillock|wooded hillocks]] and, as a recent adaptation, around [[Electricity pylon|pylons]]. In the desert areas of Namibia, they utilize [[Intermittent river|ephemeral rivers]] that [[Ephemerality|flow]] occasionally and allow large trees to grow.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Atlas" /> They usually seem to prefer desolate or protected areas. In the [[Karoo]] of [[South Africa]], they consistently avoid areas with moderate to heavy [[Agriculture|cultivation]] or with heavier or more consistent [[Wet season|winter rainfall]].<ref name="Machange">Machange, R.W., Jenkins, A.R., & Navarro, R.A. (2005). ''Eagles as indicators of ecosystem health: Is the distribution of Martial Eagle nests in the Karoo, South Africa, influenced by variations in land-use and rangeland quality?'' Journal of Arid Environments, 63(1), 223-243.</ref> One study on the occurrence of diurnal raptors in protected areas against unprotected areas found that martial eagle detection was nearly twice as frequent in [[protected area]]s during the [[dry season]] and more than three times as frequent during the wet season than in unprotected areas.<ref>Herremans, M., & Herremans-Tonnoeyr, D. (2000). ''Land use and the conservation status of raptors in Botswana''. Biological Conservation, 94(1), 31-41.</ref>
[[File:Kampfadler 02.jpg|thumb|left|Immature bird]]

==Behaviour==
The martial eagle spends an exceptional amount of the time in the air, often soaring about hill slopes high enough that binoculars are often needed to perceive them. When not breeding, both mature eagles from a breeding pair may be found roosting on their own in some prominent tree up to several miles from their nesting haunt, probably hunting for several days in one area, until viable prey resources are exhausted, and then moving on to another area.<ref name="Brown" /><ref>Pennycuick, C.J. (1972). ''Soaring behaviour and performance of some East African birds, observed from a motor‐glider''. Ibis, 114(2), 178-218.</ref> However, martial eagles, especially adult birds, are typically devoted to less disturbed areas, both due to these typically offering more extensive prey selection and their apparent dislike for a considerable human presence.<ref name="Machange" /> Martial eagles tend to be very solitary and are not known to tolerate others of the own species in the area outside of the pair during the breeding season.<ref>Ash, J., & Atkins, J. (2010). ''Birds of Ethiopia and Eritrea: an atlas of distribution''. Bloomsbury Publishing.</ref> In general this species is more shy towards humans than other big eagles of Africa, but may be seen passing over populated country at times.<ref name="Brown" /> The most frequently seen type of martial eagle away from traditional habitats are presumed nomadic subadults. One individual that was ringed as subadult was recovered 5.5 years later {{convert|130|km|mi|abbr=on}} away from the initial banding site. Another martial eagle ringed as a nestling was found to have moved {{convert|180|km|mi|abbr=on}} in 11 months.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" />

==Dietary biology==
[[File:Serengeti National Park 2021-09 - Polemaetus bellicosus - martial eagle.jpg|thumb|left|Adult martial eagle with prey.]]
The martial eagle is one of the world's most powerful avian predators. Due to both its underside spotting and ferocious efficiency as a predator it is sometimes nicknamed "the leopard of the air".<ref>Mackal, R.P. (1987). ''A Living Dinosaur?: In Search of Mokele-Mbembe''. Brill Archive.</ref> The martial eagle is an [[apex predator]], being at the top of the avian food chain in its environment.<ref name="vanEeden" /> In its common, scientific and most regional African names, this species name means "war-like" and indicates the force, brashness and indefatigable nature of their hunting habits. The aggressiveness of the hunting martial eagle, which may rival that of the overall behaviorally bolder [[crowned eagle]], can seem incongruous with their other behaviours, as it otherwise is considered a shy, wary and evasive bird.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref>Denyer, L.C.  (1960). ''Aggressive martial eagle''. Lammergeyer, 1 (1) : 43 - 44.</ref><ref>Berger, L.R., & Clarke, R.J. (1995). ''Eagle involvement in accumulation of the Taung child fauna''. Journal of Human Evolution, 29(3), 275-299.</ref> Martial eagles have been seen to charge at much larger adult ungulates and rake at their heads and flanks, at times presumably to separate the mammals from their young so they can take the latter with more ease.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://cdn1.arkive.org/media/64/64A61DA9-BBAD-4C7B-8AC6-C0D37A8A7919/Presentation.Large/Martial-eagle-attacking-warthog.jpg |title=Martial eagle attacking warthog |publisher=Arkive.org |access-date=2017-06-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180710231821/http://cdn1.arkive.org/media/64/64A61DA9-BBAD-4C7B-8AC6-C0D37A8A7919/Presentation.Large/Martial-eagle-attacking-warthog.jpg |archive-date=2018-07-10 }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |url= https://www.theguardian.com/science/grrlscientist/2012/jan/02/4 |title=Mystery bird: Martial eagle, Polemaetus bellicosus |newspaper=The Guardian |access-date=2017-06-14|date=2012-01-02}}</ref> At other times, these eagles will set down upon a wide range of potentially dangerous prey including other aggressive predators in broad daylight, such as [[monitor lizard]]s, [[venomous snake]]s, [[jackal]]s and medium-sized [[Felidae|wild cats]].<ref name="Steyn" /> Adult eagles tend to hunt larger, potentially dangerous prey more often than immature ones, presumably as they refine their hunting skills with maturity.<ref name="Steyn" /> The martial eagle hunts mostly in flight, [[Lift (soaring)|circling]] at a great height anywhere in its home range. When prey is perceived with their superb vision, the hunting eagle then stoops sharply to catch its prey by surprise with the prey often being unable to perceive the eagle at nearly as far as the eagle can perceive them despite often being in the open.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Brown" /> The martial eagle tends to hunt in a long, shallow stoop; however, when the quarry is seen in a more enclosed space, it parachutes down at a steeper angle. The speed of descent is controlled by the angle at which the wings are held above the back. At the point of impact, it shoots its long legs forward, often killing victims on impact, somewhat as large [[falcon]]s often dispatch their prey.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Brown" /> Prey may often be spotted from {{convert|3|to|5|km|mi|abbr=on}} away with a record of about {{convert|6|km|mi|abbr=on}}.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /> On occasion, they may still-hunt from a high perch or concealed in vegetation near watering holes. If the initial attempt fails, they may swoop around to attempt again, especially if the intended victim is not dangerous. If the quarry is potentially hazardous, such as [[Carnivora|mammalian carnivores]], [[venomous snake]]s or large [[ungulate]]s, and becomes aware of the eagle too soon, the eagle tends to abandon the hunt.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /> Unusually for a bird of its size, it may rarely hover while hunting. This hunting method may be employed particularly if the quarry is any of the aforementioned potentially dangerous prey items such as venomous snakes or carnivores. Other large eagles may similarly (if infrequently) hover over prey such as [[canid]]s and then quickly drop onto if the quarry makes the mistake of pointing its dangerous mouth downwards, then gripping its victim on the back while controlling the neck with the other foot until blood loss is sufficient to kill the prey.<ref>Parry-Jones, J. (1997). ''Eagle''. Dorling Kindersley Ltd.</ref><ref name="Watson" /> Prey, including birds, are generally killed on the ground, with infrequent reports of prey taken from trees. Some larger (and presumably slower-flying) avian prey may be taken while in flight; victims of successful hunts have consisted of [[water bird]]s such as [[heron]]s, [[stork]]s and [[geese]].<ref name="Steyn" /> If kills are too large and heavy to carry in flight, both members of a pair may return to the kill over several days, probably roosting nearby. If nesting, the pair tends to dismember pieces of large kills such as limbs to bring to the nest. Much of the large prey, perhaps most, that is left on the ground is lost to scavengers.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" />
[[File:Kampfadler 01.jpg|thumb|right|An adult martial eagle clutching live prey, a small [[Northern black korhaan|bustard]]]]
The diet of the martial eagle varies greatly with prey availability and can be dictated largely by opportunity. Remarkably, [[mammal]]s, [[bird]]s and [[reptile]]s can in turn dominate the prey selection of martial eagles in a given area, with no one prey type globally dominating their prey spectrum.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Brown" /> In some areas, both [[mammal]]s and [[bird]]s can each comprise more than 80% of the prey selection.<ref name="Brown" /><ref name="Macro">Boshoff, A.F., & Palmer, N.G. (1980). ''Macro-analysis of prey remains from martial eagle nests in the Cape Province''. Ostrich, 51(1), 7-13.</ref> Over 170 prey species have been reported for the martial eagle which is a much higher number than the full prey spectrum of other larger African booted eagles, and even this may neglect some of the prey they take in the little studied populations from [[West Africa|west]] and [[central Africa]] and the northern part of [[east Africa]].<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Brown2">Brown, L. (1977). ''Eagles of the World''. Universe Books.</ref> Prey may vary considerably in size but for the most part, prey weighing less than {{convert|0.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} are ignored by hunting martial eagles, with only about 15% of the known prey species averaging less than this. A majority of studies report the average size of prey for martial eagles being between {{convert|1|and|5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Boshoff">Boshoff, A.F., Palmer, N.G., & Avery, G. (1990). ''Regional variation in the diet of martial eagles in the Cape Province, South Africa''. S. Afr. J. Wildl. Res, 20, 57-68.</ref> Average weight of prey taken has been reported at as low as {{convert|1.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>Hauser, M.D. (1996). ''The Evolution of Communication''. MIT Press, Cambridge, MA.</ref> A food study largely based in data from the [[Great Rift Valley, Kenya]], as well as from [[Maasai Mara]], did reinforce a mean prey mass of just over {{convert|1.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}} for the species.<ref name="Manuel">Manuel, H. (2020). ''Nesting martial eagle (Polemaetus bellicocus) diet: Influence of prey delivery and diversity in two Kenyan ecosystems''. Biological baseline study.</ref> However, the mean prey body mass is considerably higher in other known dietary studies. In by far the largest dietary study thus far conducted for the martial eagle species (in the [[Cape Province]], [[South Africa]]) the estimated mean prey body mass was approximately {{convert|2.26|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Boshoff" /> In [[Tsavo East National Park]] of [[Kenya]], the mean estimated body mass of prey was quite similar at approximately {{convert|2.31|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Smeenk" /> Average prey weights in a study of the [[Maasai Mara]] was seemingly even higher, at a median between the average prey sizes for each sex of martial eagle at approximately {{convert|2.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Hatfield" /> Despite perhaps a majority of prey for this species weighing less than {{convert|5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, martial eagles regular prey size range is claimed at up to {{convert|12|to|15|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Cloete" /><ref name="Mooring">Mooring, M.S. (1993). ''Predation on a newborn impala by a martial eagle''. Ostrich, 64(4), 185-186.</ref><ref>Malan, G., & Crowe, T.M. (1996). ''The diet and conservation of monogamous and polyandrous pale chanting goshawks in the Little Karoo, South Africa''. South African Journal of Wildlife Research-24-month delayed open access, 26(1), 1-10.</ref> There is some evidence of prey partitioning (which can be potentially delineated both by prey species and body size of prey items taken) between the sexes. This is typical of raptors with pronounced size sexual dimorphism, as is the case in martial eagles. For instance, in populations where large adult [[monitor lizard]]s are significant as prey, they only start to appear in prey remains at nests only after the female resumes hunting in the latter part of the breeding season.<ref name="Steyn" /> The species was the focus for a study using web sourced photography to explore the species' diet across its African range, this study revealed new insight into difference in prey composition between regions, and also revealed differences in prey composition between adult and sub-adult birds, with adults found to prey more frequently on bird prey than sub-adults.<ref name="Naude">{{Cite journal|last1=Amar|first1=Arjun|last2=Krochuk|first2=Billi A.|last3=Weideman|first3=Eleanor A.|last4=Smyth|first4=Lucy K.|last5=Naude|first5=Vincent N.|date=2019-02-01|title=''Using web-sourced photography to explore the diet of a declining African raptor, the Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus)''|url=https://academic.oup.com/condor/article/121/1/duy015/5318747|journal=The Condor: Ornithological Applications|language=en|volume=121|issue=1|article-number=duy015 |doi=10.1093/condor/duy015|s2cid=146781059|issn=0010-5422|url-access=subscription}}</ref> The sexual dimorphism of kills was verified in studies from the [[Great Rift Valley]] and [[Maasai Mara]]. One study stated that the mean weight of male kills was {{convert|744|g|lb|abbr=on}} and that of females was {{convert|1.375|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Manuel" /> In Maasai Mara, the mean prey was significantly higher for both males, at about {{convert|1.98|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, and for females, at about {{convert|3.74|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Hatfield" />

===Mammals===
The most diverse class of prey in the diet as known are mammals, with over 90 mammalian prey species reported.<ref name="Steyn" /> In the [[Cape Province]], the {{convert|2.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[cape hare]] (''Lepus capensis'') reportedly dominates the prey selection, comprising about 53% of the foods selected.<ref name="Boshoff" /> Other [[lagomorph]]s, namely the slightly smaller [[Smith's red rock hare]] (''Pronolagus rupestris''), mildly larger [[African savanna hare]] (''Lepus microtis'') and the much larger {{convert|3.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[scrub hare]] (''Lepus saxatilis''), are not infrequently taken both in and outside of the Cape area.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Cloudsley-Thompson, J.L. (1990). ''Etosha and the Kaokoveld: Problems of conservation in Namibia''. Environmental conservation, 17(04), 351-354.</ref> In Maasai Mara, the most regularly identified food, at 17.3% of 191 prey items (and particularly for male martial eagles at 23.9% of their kills), was found to be comprised by a mixture of cape and scrub hares, these in total weighing an estimated mean of {{convert|2.54|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Hatfield" /> For the most part [[rodent]]s are ignored as prey as they are probably too small despite martial eagles taking at times appreciable numbers of [[Cape ground squirrel|Cape]] (''Xerus inauris'') and [[unstriped ground squirrel]]s (''Xerus rutilus'').<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>O'Shea, T. (1991). ''Xerus rutilus''. The American Society of Mammalogists, Mammalian Species No. 370: pp. 1-5.</ref><ref>Unck, C.E., Waterman, J.M., Verburgt, L., & Bateman, P.W. (2009). ''Quantity versus quality: how does level of predation threat affect Cape ground squirrel vigilance?'' Animal Behaviour, 78(3), 625-632.</ref> However, rodents selected as prey have ranged in size from the {{convert|0.14|kg|oz|abbr=on}} [[Southern African vlei rat]] (''Otomys irroratus'') to the {{convert|3.04|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[South African springhare]] (''Pedetes capensis'') and the {{convert|4|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[greater cane rat]] (''Thryonomys swinderianus'').<ref name="Steyn" /><ref>Davis, R.M. (1973). ''The ecology and life history of the vlei rat, Otomys irroratus (Brants, 1827), on the Van Riebeeck Nature Reserve, Pretoria'' (Doctoral dissertation, University of Pretoria Pretoria).</ref><ref>Mitchell, J.C.M. (1965). ''Predation on large mammals in the Kafue National Park, Zambia''. African Zoology, 1(2), 297-318.</ref> There are records of predation on {{convert|0.28|kg|oz|abbr=on}} (the second largest African bat) [[straw-coloured fruit bat]]s (''Eidolon helvum'') and [[galago]]s of various sizes (usually weighing a kilo or less) but otherwise mammalian prey they pursue tends to be relatively larger.<ref>Mikula, P., Morelli, F., Lučan, R.K., Jones, D.N., & Tryjanowski, P. (2016). ''Bats as prey of diurnal birds: a global perspective''. Mammal Review.</ref><ref>Bearder, S.K. (2007). ''A comparison of calling patterns in two nocturnal primates, Otolemur crassicaudatus and Galago moholi as a guide to predation risk''. In Primate Anti-predator Strategies (pp. 206-221). Springer US.</ref><ref>Rainier of Monaco, GH Bourne (Eds.), (1977). ''Primate Conservation''. Academic Press, New York.</ref>
[[File:Lepus capensis.jpg|thumb|left|[[Cape hare]] are the main prey for martial eagles in the [[Cape Province]] of [[South Africa]].]]
Locally, large numbers are taken of any species of [[hyrax]]. The attractiveness of hyraxes as a prey resource may encourage martial eagles to vary their hunting techniques to potentially more time-consuming perch hunting so that they may capture [[rock hyrax]]es from rock formations and [[tree hyrax]]es from trees, contrary to their usual preference for capturing prey on the ground in the open after soaring high. Ranging in average mass from {{convert|2.2|to|3.14|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, hyraxes can comprise a healthy meal for a family of martial eagle and are probably among the larger items that male eagles will regularly deliver to nests.<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Hoeck, H. (2001). ''Hyraxes''. Pp. 448-451 in D Macdonald, S Norris, eds. ''The New Encyclopedia of Mammals''. Oxford, UK: Oxford University Press.</ref><ref name="Kingdon">Kingdon, J., Happold, D., Hoffman, M., Butynski, T., Happold, M., & Kalina, J. (2013). ''Mammals of Africa: 6 Vols''. A & C Black.</ref><ref>Barry, R.E., & [[Jeheskel Shoshani|Shoshani, J]]. (2000). ''Heterohyrax brucei''. Mammalian species, 1-7.</ref> Another miscellaneous mammal known to fall prey to martial eagles is the [[ground pangolin]] (''Smutsia temminckii''), although it is not clear the age pangolins that are preyed on and how they are dispatched, considering that adults weigh some {{convert|11.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and have a hard keratin shell that is capable of withstanding [[lion]] (''Panthera leo'') jaws when in its rolled-up defensive posture.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Simmons" /><ref>Swart, J.M., Richardson, P.R.K. & Ferguson, J.W.H. ''Ecological factors affecting the feeding behaviour of pangolins (Manis temminckii)''. Journal of Zoology 247.3 (1999): 281-292.</ref> 

Although far less accomplished and prolific as a predator of [[Old World monkeys|monkeys]] than the [[crowned eagle]], the martial eagle has been known to prey on at least 14 species of monkey. The monkeys to turn up most often as martial eagle prey are [[grivet]]s (''Chlorocebus aethiops''), [[vervet monkey]]s (''Chlorocebus pygerythrus'') and [[malbrouck]]s (''Chlorocebus cynosuros''), with mean body masses of {{convert|2.8|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, {{convert|4.12|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and {{convert|4.53|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, respectively, because of their savanna-woods dwelling habits, tendencies to forage on the ground and their primarily diurnal activity.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Smeenk" /><ref name="KV2">Kingdon, Jonathan. Mammals of Africa: Volume II: Primates. A&C Black, 2014.</ref><ref>Cawthorn Lang, K. 2001. "Primate Factsheets: Vervet (Chlorocebus) Taxonomy" (On-line).</ref> These monkey species have special alarm calls, distinct from those uttered in response to the presence of for example a [[leopard]] (''Panthera pardus''), specifically for martial eagles.<ref>Seyfarth, R.M., Cheney, D.L., & Marler, P. (1980). ''Monkey responses to three different alarm calls: evidence of predator classification and semantic communication''. Science, 210(4471), 801-803.</ref><ref>Cheney, D.L., & Seyfarth, R.M. (1981). ''Selective forces affecting the predator alarm calls of vervet monkeys''. Behaviour, 76(1), 25-60.</ref> Martial eagles are also known to prey on {{convert|3.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[lesser spot-nosed monkey]]s (''Cercopithecus petaurista''), {{convert|4.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Lowe's mona monkey]]s (''Cercopithecus lowei''),<ref name="KV2" /><ref>Delson, E., C.J. Terranova, W.L. Jungers, E.J. Sargis, N.G. Jablonski, & P.C. Dechow. 2000. Body mass in Cercopithecidae (Primates, Mammalia): Estimation and scaling in extinct and extant taxa. American Museum of Natural History, Anthropological Papers No. 83. 159 pp</ref> and possibly larger [[Tana River mangabey]]s (''Cercocebus galeritus'') and [[red colobus]] (''colobus badius'').<ref>Stanford, C.B., Wallis, J., Matama, H., & Goodall, J. (1994).  ''Patterns of predation by chimpanzees on red colobus monkeys in Gombe National Park, 1982–1991''. American Journal of Physical Anthropology, 94(2), 213-228.</ref><ref>Stanford, Craig B. "The influence of chimpanzee predation on group size and anti-predator behaviour in red colobus monkeys." Animal Behaviour 49.3 (1995): 577-587.</ref> Predatory attacks on juvenile [[baboon]]s (''Papio sp.'') by martial eagles have been reported, especially [[olive baboon]]s (''Papio anubis'').<ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="KV2" /><ref>Pereira, M.E. (1988). ''Effects of age and sex on intra-group spacing behaviour in juvenile savannah baboons, Papio cynocephalus cynocephalus''. Animal behaviour, 36(1), 184-204.</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Polemaetus_bellicosus/ | title=Polemaetus bellicosus (Martial eagle) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]]}}</ref> On at least one occasion, this species has attacked humans with apparently predatory intent, making it one of the only birds of prey to do so. In 2019 a young boy was killed and two other children injured by an immature martial eagle in the Ethiopian town of [[Gaashaamo]].<ref>{{cite book |title=The Deadly Balance: Predators and People in a Crowded World | first = Adam | last = Hart | date = 2023 | publisher= Bloomsbury |chapter-url=https://lithub.com/this-eagle-would-eat-your-toddler-if-it-had-the-chance/ | chapter=This Eagle Would Eat Your Toddler If It Had The Chance | type= excerpt via ''Literary Hub'' |isbn=978-1-4729-8532-3 | url= https://books.google.com/books?id=oEmOEAAAQBAJ}}</ref>

A wide range of [[Carnivora|mammalian carnivores]] are important prey for martial eagles. Among these many [[mongoose]] tend to be well represented in their diet. Most mongooses native to the savanna tend to be highly social burrowers. The martial eagle is a known predator of the full size range of mongoose species, from the smallest species, the {{convert|0.27|kg|oz|abbr=on}} [[common dwarf mongoose]] (''Helogale parvula''), to the largest, the {{convert|4.14|kg|lb|abbr=on}} adult [[white-tailed mongoose]] (''Ichneumia albicauda'').<ref name="Kingdon" /><ref name="Naude" /><ref name="Skinner">Skinner, J. D., & Chimimba, C. T. (2005). The mammals of the southern African sub-region. Cambridge University Press.</ref> However, most of these types of mongoose are also relatively small, and can effectively escape quickly to the safety of their underground home, so the lighter, more nimble male martial eagle is more likely to habitually pursue them. In southern Africa, the {{convert|0.72|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[meerkat]] (''Suricata suricatta'') comprises up to at least 9.6% of prey remains (as in the [[Cape Province]]) and the {{convert|0.75|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Cape grey mongoose]] (''Galerella pulverulenta'') comprising an average of 7.2% of prey remains in the Cape area.<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Cavallini, P. (1992). ''Herpestes pulverulentas''. Mammalian Species Archive, 409, 1-4.</ref><ref>Rood, J.P. (1986). ''Ecology and social evolution in the mongooses''. Ecological aspects of social evolution, 131, 152.</ref> In pooled data from the Great Rift Valley and Maasai Mara, the [[banded mongoose]] (''Mungos mungo''), the largest of the social savanna-dwelling mongoose at {{convert|2.12|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, fell third behind only francolins and hares as the most regularly selected prey for martial eagles.<ref name="Manuel" /> Despite often being successful in capturing banded mongoose, in one case when an immature martial eagle took one to a tree, the dominant male banded mongoose of the group scaled the tree and pulled the still-living mongoose prey to safety.<ref>Cant, M.A., Nichols, H.J., Thompson, F.J., & Vitikainen, E.I.K. (2016). ''Banded mongooses: demography, life history, and social behavior''. Cooperative Breeding in Vertebrates, 318-337.</ref><ref>Rood, J.P. (1983). ''Banded mongoose rescues pack member from eagle''. Animal Behaviour, 31(4), 1261-1262.</ref> Other similar-sized carnivores known to fall prey to martial eagles include {{convert|0.83|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[striped polecat]] (''Ictonyx striatus'') and a few species of [[Genet (animal)|genet]], which are about twice as heavy on average as the polecat.<ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Kingdon" /> Canids are also readily preyed upon. In the [[Cape Province]], 72 [[bat-eared fox]]es (''Otocyon megalotis''), which average about {{convert|4.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, were found in the prey remains, 85% of which were adults.<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Kingdon" /> 5 adult [[Cape fox]] (''Vulpes chama''), around {{convert|2.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}} were also taken.<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Maxime Lavoie, Aurélie Renard, Justin A Pitt, Serge Larivière, Vulpes chama (Carnivora: Canidae), Mammalian Species, Volume 51, Issue 972, 5 July 2019, Pages 11–17, https://doi.org/10.1093/mspecies/sez002</ref> Even larger [[black-backed jackal]]s (''Canis mesomelas'') are occasionally targeted as prey, especially young pups.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref>Sillero-Zubiri, C., Hoffmann, M., & Macdonald, D.W. (Eds.). (2004). ''Canids: foxes, wolves, jackals and dogs: status survey and conservation action plan'' (pp. x+-430). IUCN.</ref><ref>Lawick, H. V., & Goodall, J. (1970). Innocent killers.</ref> Adult [[domestic dog]]s (''Canis familiaris'') of up to a modest size may occasionally be killed by martial eagles.<ref name="Govender">Govender, B. (2015). ''Waterfall pets fall prey to eagles''. Highway Mail, Zimbabwe.</ref> Martial eagles are also known to opportunistically grab pups of [[African wild dog]]s (''Lycaon pictus'') as they emerge from their dens.<ref>Jackman, B., & Scott, J. (2012). ''The marsh lions: the story of an African pride''. Bradt Travel Guides.</ref> Although less frequently taken, a few species of [[felid]]s have been included in their prey spectrum. Adults of both [[Cat|domestic cat]]s and their ancestors, the {{convert|4.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[African wildcat]] (''Felis lybica''), are known to fall prey to this species.<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Govender" /> Much larger cat species such as [[serval]]s (''Leptailurus serval'') and [[caracal]]s (''Caracal caracal'') are known to be taken, mainly kittens but even reportedly adults.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Kingdon" /><ref name="Macro" /><ref>Hatfield, R. S., Davis, A. G., Buij, R., Cox, J. J., Kapila, S., Parmuntoro, L., ... & van Langevelde, F. (2024). Africa's overlooked top predator: Towards a better understanding of martial eagle feeding ecology in the Maasai Mara, Kenya. Wildlife Biology, e01223.</ref> Apparent predatory attacks are even attempted on [[big cat]] cubs as they are considered potential predators of [[leopard]] (''Panthera pardus'') cubs and confirmed predators of [[lion]] and [[cheetah]] (''Acinonyx jubatus'') cubs. However, they rapidly abandon hunting attempts if the formidable mother lion or leopard is present.<ref>Eloff, F.C. (1980). ''Cub mortality in the Kalahari lion Panthera leo vernayi (Roberts, 1948)''. Koedoe, 23(1), 163-170.</ref><ref>[[Tim Caro|Caro, T. M.]] (1987). ''Cheetah mothers' vigilance: looking out for prey or for predators?'' Behavioral Ecology and Sociobiology, 20(5), 351-361.</ref><ref>Scott, J. & Scott, A. (2013). ''The Leopard's Tale: Featuring Half-Tail and Zawadi, Stars of Big Cat Diary''. Bradt Travel Narratives, {{ISBN|978-1841624792}}.</ref><ref>{{cite web |url= https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3vttLhONIc0  |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211221/3vttLhONIc0 |archive-date=2021-12-21 |url-status=live|title= Lion cubs play when Martial Eagle attempts snatch 1223 - Kicheche Mara Camp, Mara North Conservancy |date=6 October 2016 |publisher=YouTube |access-date=2017-06-14}}{{cbignore}}</ref><ref>Hatfield, R. S., Parmuntoro, L., Thomsett, S., Reynolds, P., & Elliot, N. B. (2024). Predator becomes prey: Martial eagle predation of lion cubs in the greater Mara region, Kenya. ''Ecology and Evolution'', 14, e70148. https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.70148</ref> Successful predatory attacks on other carnivores have included small [[African civet]] (''Civettictis civetta'') and the juvenile [[aardwolf]] (''Proteles cristata'').<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Maugham, R.C.F. (1914). ''Wild game in Zambezia''. J. Murray.</ref>
[[File:Damara Dik-Dik.JPG|thumb|right|[[Kirk's dik-dik]] are one of the two main prey species for martial eagles in [[Tsavo East National Park]], [[Kenya]].]]
While large accipitrids worldwide are credited with attacks on young or small [[ungulate]]s, perhaps no other species is as accomplished in this regard as this martial eagle. Over 30 species of ungulate have been identified as prey for this species, more species than are attributed to the perhaps more powerful [[crowned eagle]]s and all the world's [[golden eagle]]s. However, in all three, ungulates in a given region comprise more than 30% of the diet.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Smeenk" /><ref name="Watson" /><ref name="Boshoff" /> In [[Kruger National Park]], the martial eagle is mentioned as the only bird considered as a major predator of ungulate species.<ref name="Pienaar">{{cite journal|author=Pienaar, U. D. V. |title= Predator-prey relationships amongst the larger mammals of the Kruger National Park|journal= Koedoe|volume= 12|doi=10.4102/koedoe.v12i1.753|year= 1969|doi-access= free}}</ref> A majority of the ungulate diet of martial eagles are comprised by small [[antelope]] species or the young of larger antelopes. In Maasai Mara, young ungulates appeared particularly significant in the diet of adult female martial eagles, with [[impala]] (''Aepyceros melampus'') fawns averaging an estimated {{convert|7.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}} comprising 34.2% of female kills (and 13.6% of the species' overall foods) and [[Thomson's gazelle]] (''Eudorcas thomsonii'') fawns at about {{convert|3.75|kg|lb|abbr=on}} comprising a further 15.1% of female kills (and 10.5% of the overall diet here). Furthermore, young [[Grant's gazelle]]s (''Nanger granti''), weighing a mean of {{convert|9.02|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, were sometimes taken by females in Maasai Mara.<ref name="Hatfield" /> For the newborn impala, weighing already {{convert|5.55|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, the martial eagle is the only bird considered to be a significant predator.<ref name="Mooring" /><ref>Moe, S.R., Rutina, L.P., & Du Toit, J.T. (2007). ''Trade‐off between resource seasonality and predation risk explains reproductive chronology in impala''. Journal of Zoology, 273(3), 237-243.</ref> Calves of the other antelope may also be included in their prey spectrum, including [[hartebeest]] (''Alcelaphus buselaphus''), [[bontebok]] (''Damaliscus pygargus''), [[common tsessebe]] (''Damaliscus lunatus''), [[springbok]] (''Antidorcas marsupialis''), ''[[Eudorcas]]'' [[gazelle]]s, [[gerenuk]] (''Litocranius walleri''), [[Cape bushbuck|bushbuck]] (''Tragelaphus sylvaticus''), [[grey rhebok]] (''Pelea capreolus''), [[kob]] (''Kobus kob'') and [[mountain reedbuck]] (''Redunca arundinum''). These species can vary in weight from {{convert|2.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}} (i.e. gazelles) to {{convert|11|kg|lb|abbr=on}} (i.e. tsessebe) in newborns.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Smeenk" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Joubert, S.C.J. (1972). ''Territorial behaviour of the tsessebe (''Damaliscus lunatus lunatus'' Burchell) in the Kruger National Park''. African Zoology, 7(1), 141-156.</ref><ref>Elliott, C.C.H. (1984). ''Martial Eagle Polemaetus bellicosus attacking Hartebeest calf Alcephalus buselaphus''. Scopus, 8: 45.</ref><ref>Modha, K.L., & Eltringham, S.K. (1976). ''Population ecology of the Uganda kob (Adenota kob thomasi (Neumann)) about the territorial system in the Rwenzori National Park, Uganda''. Journal of applied ecology, 453-473.</ref><ref>Roberts, S.C., & Dunbar, R.I. (1991). ''Climatic influences on the behavioural ecology of Chanter's mountain reedbuck in Kenya''. African Journal of Ecology, 29(4), 316-329.</ref><ref>MacLeod, J.G.R., Murray, E.M., & Murray, C.D.C. (1952). ''The Birds of the Hottentots Holland (Part II)''. Ostrich, 23(1), 16-25.</ref> Additionally, piglets of [[warthog]]s (''Phacochoerus africanus'') (of which only the martial eagle among accipitrids is similarly mentioned as a significant predator) and [[bushpig]]s (''Potamochoerus larvatus'') are taken.<ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Somers, M.J., & Penzhorn, B.L. (1992). ''Reproduction in a reintroduced warthog population in the eastern Cape Province''. South African Journal of Wildlife Research, 22, 57-57.</ref> 

While martial eagles usually tend to focus on young fawns, adults of small antelopes are also attacked and taken as prey. Locally favored prey are the [[dik-dik]]s, one of the smallest kinds of antelope, and every known species may be vulnerable to this eagle.<ref name="Castelló">Castelló, J.R. (2016). ''Bovids of the World: Antelopes, Gazelles, Cattle, Goats, Sheep, and Relatives''. Princeton University Press.</ref> In [[Tsavo East National Park]], [[Kirk's dik-dik]] (''Madoqua kirkii'') were the second most numerous prey species and it was estimated that at least 86 dik-diks are taken in the park over the year by two pairs of martial eagles. At an average of {{convert|5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, these can provide a very fulfilling meal for an eagle family.<ref name="Smeenk" /> Adults of other small antelope such as {{convert|4.95|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[suni]] (''Neotragus moschatus'') and {{convert|4.93|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[blue duiker]]s (''Philantomba monticola'') are probably also taken with relative ease.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Kingdon" /> Larger species weighing between {{convert|7|and|15|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in adults, are known to be preyed upon, including both species of [[Raphicerus|grysbok]], [[steenbok]]s (''Raphicerus campestris''), [[klipspringer]]s (''Oreotragus oreotragus'') [[red forest duiker]]s (''Cephalophus natalensis''), and [[oribi]]s (''Ourebia ourebi'').<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Pienaar" /><ref name="Castelló" /><ref name="Skinner" /><ref name="Irwin">Irwin, M.P.S. (1981). ''The birds of Zimbabwe''. Quest Publishing, Salisbury, Zimbabwe.</ref><ref>Pooley, A.C. (1970). ''Powerful eagle-Martial eagle kills red duiker''. Lammegeyer, 11: 82.</ref> The largest prey taken by martial eagles are [[common duiker]] (''Sylvicapra grimmia''), potentially weighing from {{convert|15|to|25|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in weight.<ref name="Simmons" /><ref>Furstenburg, D. Common duiker (Grey duiker, Bush duiker, Grimm's duiker) Sylvicapra grimmia (Linnaeus, 1758).</ref> However, one adult female duiker dispatched via strangulation weighed an estimated {{convert|37|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, one of the largest known raptorial kill for any species on the [[Africa]]n continent.<ref name="Steyn" />

Among extant birds of prey, only [[wedge-tailed eagle]]s, reportedly capable of killing [[sheep]] and female [[red kangaroo]]s (''Macropus rufus'') weighing up to {{convert|50|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, [[crowned eagle]]s taking [[Cape bushbuck|antelope]] of the same estimated weight and [[golden eagle]]s, credited with taking adult female [[deer]] of several species with weights estimated at {{convert|50|to|70|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, and capable of apparently dispatching domestic calves weighing up to {{convert|114|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, have larger kills attributed to them.<ref>Olsen, P. (2005). ''The Wedge-tailed Eagle''. CSIRO Publishing (Australian Natural History Series), {{ISBN|978-0643091658}}.</ref><ref>Nybakk, K., Kjelvik, O. & Kvam, T. 1999. ''Golden Eagle predation on semidomestic reindeer''. Wildlife Society Bulletin, 27 (4): 1038-1042.</ref><ref>Kerley, L.L., & Slaght, J.C. (2013). ''First documented predation of Sika deer (''Cervus nippon'') by Golden Eagle (Aquila chrysaetos) in Russian far east''. Journal of Raptor Research, 47(3), 328-330.</ref><ref>Phillips, R.L., Cummings, J.L., Notah, G. & Mullis, C. 1996. ''Golden eagle predation on domestic calves''. Wildlife Society Bulletin, 24: 468-470.</ref><ref>Kemp, A. C., G. M. Kirwan, and J. S. Marks (2020). ''Crowned Eagle (Stephanoaetus coronatus)'', version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref>

===Birds===
[[File:Helmeted Guineafowl, Numida meleagris at Marakele National Park, South Africa (14155488202).jpg|thumb|left|[[Helmeted guineafowl]] are among the most favored prey species for martial eagles, known to be taken in considerable numbers everywhere from [[West Africa]] to [[South Africa]].]]
Compared to the range and sizes of mammals included in their prey spectrum, birds taken by martial eagles may seem less impressive as a whole, but the morphology of the martial eagle, including large wing surface areas, pronounced sexual dimorphism and relatively long toes, shows that the species is at least partially specialized to hunt avian prey. Birds  are universally considered by biologists more difficult to capture than mammals of the same size. In all, more than 50 bird species have been identified as the prey of martial eagles.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref>Temeles, E.J. (1985). ''Sexual size dimorphism of bird-eating hawks: the effect of prey vulnerability''. The American Naturalist, 125(4), 485-499.</ref> The most significant portion of the avian diet is comprised by medium-sized terrestrial upland birds such as [[guineafowl]], [[Pternistis|spurfowl]], [[Francolinus|francolin]]s<ref>{{cite web | url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Polemaetus_bellicosus/ |title=Polemaetus bellicosus (Martial eagle) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]]}}</ref> and [[bustard]]s. In total more than a dozen species of the [[Galliformes|galliform]] order and the bustard family each have been identified as their prey.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="Boshoff" /> When attacking these ground-loving birds, which are understandably quite easily spooked and usually react to potential danger by flying off, martial eagles almost always try to take them on the ground much like they do mammalian prey. If the birds take flight, the hunting attempt will fail, although a hunting eagle may try to surprise the same birds again.<ref name="Steyn" /> In [[Niger]], the most numerous prey species is apparently the {{convert|1.29|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[helmeted guineafowl]] (''Numida meleagris'').<ref>Thiollay, J. (2006). ''The decline of raptors in West Africa: long‐term assessment and the role of protected areas''. Ibis, 148(2), 240-254.</ref> Other guineafowl such as the [[Vulturine guineafowl|vulturine]] (''Acryllium vulturinum'') and [[crested guineafowl]] (''Guttera edouardi'') are also readily taken elsewhere.<ref>van Niekerk, J.H. (2015). ''Vocal behavior of Crested Guineafowl (Guttera edouardi) based on visual and sound playback surveys in the Umhlanga Lagoon Nature Reserve, KwaZulu-Natal province, South Africa''. Avian Research, 6(1), 13.</ref><ref>http://l7.alamy.com/zooms/a8683b6f93e84a78af9c3a43fd22c468/martial-eagle-eating-vulturine-guineafowl-samburu-kenya-bg92xd.jpg . Retrieved 2017.</ref> Guineafowl and spurfowl were stated as the most numerous prey for martial eagles in [[Kruger National Park]].<ref name="Kruger">{{cite web|title=Kruger Park Birding: Eagles and Hawk-Eagles Birding Raptor Guide|url=http://birding.krugerpark.co.za/birding-in-kruger-raptor-eagles.html|work=Kruger National Park - Bird Education|publisher=Siyabona Africa: Your Informed African Travel Partner|access-date=9 December 2011}}</ref> In [[Tsavo East National Park]], the {{convert|0.67|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[red-crested korhaan]] (''Lophotis ruficrista''), perhaps the smallest bustard the eagle hunts, is the most numerous prey taken, comprising about 39% of the prey remains.<ref name="Smeenk" /> In the Great Rift Valley and Maasai Mara data, the [[Coqui francolin]] (''Peliperdix coqui'') was reportedly the most regularly identified prey and, in separate studies, helmeted guineafowl averaging {{convert|1.48|kg|lb|abbr=on}} made up 12% of the foods in Maasai Mara.<ref name="Hatfield" /><ref name="Manuel" /> Medium-sized bustards such as the {{convert|1.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Hartlaub's bustard]] (''Lissotis hartlaubii'') and the {{convert|1.7|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[karoo korhaan]] (''Eupodotis vigorsii'') were oft-taken supplemental prey in Tsavo East and the Cape Province, respectively.<ref name="Smeenk" /><ref name="CRC2" /><ref name="Boshoff" /> Although these are not usually taken in large numbers, martial eagles are one of the main predators of larger bustards. These may include (averaged between the extremely size dimorphic sexes) the {{convert|3.44|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Ludwig's bustard]] (''Neotis ludwigii''), the {{convert|5.07|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Denham's bustard]] (''Neotis denhami'') and even the [[kori bustard]] (''Ardeotis kori''), seemingly the heaviest bustard in the world on average at {{convert|8.43|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="CRC2" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Naude" /> Attacks on adult male kori bustards, which are certain to be the largest avian prey attacked by martial eagles and are twice as heavy as females, averaging some {{convert|11.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, can be extremely prolonged. One protracted battle resulted in an injured leg for the eagle and massive, fatal blood loss for the male bustard, which was ultimately scavenged by a jackal by the following morning.<ref name="CRC2" />
[[File:Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus) (46549231181).jpg|thumb|right|Martial eagle eating a kill.]]
Despite its preference for ground-dwelling avian prey, a surprisingly considerable number of water birds may also be attacked. [[Waterfowl]] known to be attacked include the {{convert|1.18|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[South African shelduck]] (''Tadorna cana''), {{convert|1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[yellow-billed duck]] (''Anas undulata''), the {{convert|4.43|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[spur-winged goose]] (''Plectropterus gambensis'') (Africa's largest waterfowl species) and especially the peculiar, overly bold and aggressive {{convert|1.76|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Egyptian goose]] (''Alopochen aegyptiaca''), which is one of the main prey species for martial eagles in [[Kruger National Park]].<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="CRC2" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Kruger" /><ref>Halse, S.A., & Skead, D.M. (1983). ''Wing moult, body measurements and condition indices of spur-winged geese''. Wildfowl, 34(34), 108-114.</ref> Based on the high estimated weight when taken of Egyptian goose of {{convert|3.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, male geese may be targeted over females.<ref name="Naude" /> Larger wading birds are also fairly frequently attacked including [[heron]]s and [[egret]]s, [[flamingo]]es [[stork]]s, [[ibis]]es, [[spoonbill]]s and [[Crane (bird)|crane]]s.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Long, S. (1998). ''Tree-nesting Crowned Crane and response to potential predators''. Honeyguide 44: 143.</ref><ref>{{cite news |url= http://www.gettyimages.com/detail/photo/martial-eagle-plucks-its-prey-a-lesser-high-res-stock-photography/139803119?esource=SEO_GIS_CDN_Redirect |title= MARTIAL EAGLE (POLEMAETUS BELLICOSUS) PLUCKS ITS PREY, A LESSER FLAMINGO. NGORONGORO CRATER. |author= Fred Bruemmer |newspaper= Getty Images |access-date= 2017-06-14 |archive-date= 2018-07-25 |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20180725122815/https://www.gettyimages.com/detail/photo/martial-eagle-plucks-its-prey-a-lesser-high-res-stock-photography/139803119?esource=SEO_GIS_CDN_Redirect |url-status= dead }}</ref> The diversity and number of storks taken is particularly impressive. They are known to take 8 species of stork, ranging from the smallest known species, the {{convert|1.08|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[African openbill]] (''Anastomus lamelligerus''), to the tallest species in the world, the {{convert|6.16|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, {{convert|1.5|m|ftin|0|abbr=on}}-tall [[saddle-billed stork]] (''Ephippiorhynchus senegalensis''). One naturalists observed up to a half dozen attacks in different parts of Africa on {{convert|3.45|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[white stork]]s (''Ciconia ciconia'').<ref name="Steyn" /><ref>Steyn, P. (1987). ''Martial Eagle kills Woolly-necked Stork''. African Wildlife 41: 147.</ref><ref>Curry-Lindahl, K. (1971). ''Prey of the Martial Eagle''. Africana, 4 (5): 8.</ref> Short of three attacks on [[spotted thick-knee]]s (''Burhinus capensis''), which weigh about {{convert|0.42|kg|oz|abbr=on}}, and smaller still [[crowned lapwing]]s (''Vanellus coronatus'') so far as is known small waders or shorebirds are ignored as prey.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Hatfield" /><ref name="Boshoff" /> Other assorted avian prey may consists of [[ostrich]] (''Struthio camelus'') chicks weighing an estimated mean of {{convert|4.5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, frequently resulting in the immediate ire of protective ostrich parents. Further avian prey may extent to [[sandgrouse]], [[pigeon]]s and [[dove]]s, [[hornbill]]s and [[crow]]s.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Simmons" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Naude" /><ref>Tree, A.J. (2014). ''Ostrich chicks as prey of Martial Eagle''. Honeyguide. 60(1): 56.</ref> Beyond occasional captures of other [[birds of prey]] (covered later), one other impressive avian prey species is the [[southern ground hornbill]] (''Bucorvus leadbeateri''), which at {{convert|3.77|kg|lb|abbr=on}} is probably the world's largest hornbill.<ref name="CRC2" /><ref>Kemp, M.I. (1980). ''The biology of the Southern ground hornbill Bucorvus leadbeateri (Vigors)(Aves: Bucerotidae)''. Annals of the Transvaal Museum, 32(4), 35-247.</ref> At the other end of the scale, some martial eagles may capture a few small social species of passerine, which are exceptionally small prey (the smallest recorded prey species for the eagle overall), potentially consisting of the {{convert|18.6|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[red-billed quelea]]s (''Quelea quelea'') and the {{convert|27.4|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[sociable weaver]]s (''Philetairus socius''), as practically every meat-eating bird in Africa may be attracted to these species' colonial abundance.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="CRC2" /><ref>Maclean, G.L. (1973). ''The sociable weaver, part 4: predators, parasites and symbionts''. Ostrich, 44(3-4), 241-253.</ref>

===Reptiles===
[[File:Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus) with its prey - a White-throated Monitor Lizard (16844993595).jpg|thumb|left|Large monitors, such as this prey, a [[rock monitor]], can be important prey for adult female martial eagles.]]
Reptiles can be locally important in the diet, and they are known to take larger numbers of reptiles than other large African booted eagles. Only relatively large reptiles, it seems, are attacked and many of this prey is also potentially dangerous, so martial eagles have unusually long, gracile tarsi, perhaps an adaptation to dangerous large reptilian prey.<ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Lerner" /> Based on the photographic analysis, reptilian prey, predominately adult monitors comprise 21% of the total 239 prey items.<ref name="Naude" /> In particular, in the former [[Transvaal province]] of northeastern South Africa, reptiles were the main prey, with [[monitor lizard]]s alone comprising just under half of the prey remains.<ref name="Tarboton">Tarboton, W.R. & Allan, D.G. (1984). ''The status and conservation of birds of prey in the Transvaal''. Transvaal Museum Monograph No. 3. Pretoria.</ref> A small food study in [[Zimbabwe]] found that 69% of 39 prey items were made up of monitor lizards.<ref>Kemp, A. C., P. F. D. Boesman, and J. S. Marks (2020). Martial Eagle (Polemaetus bellicosus), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.mareag1.01</ref> Large monitors such as [[rock monitor]]s (''Varanus albigularis''), the [[nile monitor]]s (''Varanus niloticus'') and the [[savannah monitor]]s (''Varanus exanthematicus'') are mainly taken.<ref name="Hatfield" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="KS">{{cite web|title=Eagle Rips Lizard's Eyes Apart As it Tries to Escape|url=https://latestsightings.com/single-post/eagle-rips-lizards-eyes-apart-as-it-tries-to-escape-kruger-national-park|publisher=Kruger sighting|date=September 15, 2020}}</ref> These monitors are the largest lizards in Africa, weighing roughly around {{convert|3.1|to|6.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}} in standard adults, can be a formidable but fulfilling prey.<ref>Auliya, Mark, and Andé Koch. Visual identification guide to the monitor lizard species of the world (genus Varanus). Bundesamt für Naturschutz, 2020.</ref><ref>de Buffrenil, V. & G. Hemer. 2002. Variation in longevity, growth, and morphology in exploited Nile monitors (Varanus niloticus) from Sahelian Africa. Journal of Herpetology 36: 419-426.</ref><ref>Phillips, John A. "Movement Patterns and Density of Varanus Albigularis." Journal of Herpetology, vol. 29, no. 3, 1995, pp. 407–16. JSTOR, https://doi.org/10.2307/1564991. Accessed 10 Feb. 2023.</ref> So most of the hunts are ambushes on adult monitors by mature female eagles.<ref name="Smeenk" /><ref name="Boshoff" /><ref name="Simmons" /> Sometimes a lengthy struggle will ensue as the eagles try to get a good grip on the tough back skin of the monitors while simultaneously trying to control their necks to avoid the prey's powerful jaws, however, the eagles are usually successful in dispatching the large lizards.<ref name="KS" /><ref name="Middleton">Middleton, A.G. (2002). ''Feeding ecology of some eagles on Triangle Estate''. Honeyguide. 48(2): 181-189.</ref>

Other reptiles are also occasionally taken. In [[Kruger National Park]], reptiles as a whole made up 38% of the prey remains. These consisted of monitor lizards as well as a wide range of [[snake]]s.<ref name="Kruger" /> Elsewhere, [[snouted cobra]]s (''Naja annulifera'') may added to the list of their prey spectrum.<ref name="Simmons" /> Even youngsters of the [[African rock python]] (''Python sebae''), the largest African snake was taken.<ref name="Naude" /> [[Tent tortoise]]s (''Psammobates tentorius'') and possibly [[Bell's hinge-back tortoise]]s (''Kinixys spekii'') are also taken by martial eagles,<ref name="Boshoff" /><ref>Lloyd, D.A. (1998). ''Predation on the tent tortoise Psammobates tentorius: a whodunit with the honey badger Mellivora capensis as prime suspect''. African Zoology, 33(4), 200-202.</ref><ref>Hailey, A. (2001). ''Low survival rate and high predation in the African hingeback tortoise Kinixys spekii''. African Journal of Ecology, 39(4), 383-392.</ref> In one case, an estimated {{convert|90|cm|ftin|0|abbr=on}} [[nile crocodile]] (''Crocodylus niloticus'') was captured and flown with by a martial eagle.<ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.astepahead.es/always-something-new/ |title=Always Something New |publisher= Astepahead Custom Guided Journeys |access-date=2017-06-14|date=2010-10-16}}</ref>

===Interspecies predatory relationships===
[[File:Martial Eagle - Mara - Kenya S4E7401 (15526799382).jpg|thumb|Adult in Masai Mara, Kenya.]]
For terrestrial predators, including birds of prey, sub-Saharan Africa may be the most competitive environment in the modern world. Due to great diversity of raptors present, each species have shown adaptive specializations, which may consist of various morphological differences that allow them to capitalize on distinct prey selection, hunting methods, habitat and/or nesting habits.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref>Kemp, A., & Kemp, M. (1998). ''Sasol Birds of Prey: Of Africa and Its Islands''. New Holland.</ref><ref>Mills, M.G.L., & Biggs, H. C. (1993). ''Prey apportionment and related ecological relationships between large carnivores in Kruger National Park''. In Symposia of the Zoological Society of London (Vol. 65, pp. 253-268).</ref> The larger booted eagles that dominate the avian food chain in Africa consists of martial eagles, {{convert|4|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[Verreaux's eagle]]s and {{convert|3.64|kg|lb|abbr=on}} [[crowned eagle]]s, which due to their size and conspicuousness may lend themselves to comparisons. While prey species may overlap in these in [[southern Africa]] and some parts of [[east Africa]], where the prey size range of all three eagles averages {{convert|1|to|5|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, these three powerful eagles differ considerably in habitat preferences, nesting habits and hunting methods. The Verreaux's eagle nests in and hunts around rocky, mountainous [[Inselberg|kopje]] to be in close proximity to the much favored prey, [[rock hyrax]]es, which they mainly use contour-hunting (hugging the uneven ground to surprise the prey) to capture. The crowned eagle dwells mainly in mature forests, building nests in large interior trees, and is primarily a perch-hunter, watching and listening for monkeys and other prey over a long period. While all three are known to locally favor rock hyraxes, the nesting habitat differences where they overlap are sufficient to allow these birds not to effect one another.<ref name="RaptorsWorld" /><ref name="Steyn" /><ref name="Mendelsohn" /><ref name="CRC" /><ref name="CRC2" /><ref>Brown, L.H. (1953). ''On the biology of the large birds of prey of the Embu district, Kenya Colony''. Ibis, 95(1), 74-114.</ref> The average prey mass of Verreaux's eagle was similar to that martial eagles, with a pair of studies showing it ranges from {{convert|1.82|to|2.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Watson" /><ref>{{cite web |url= http://www.africanraptors.org/the-verreauxs-eagle-an-interview-with-dr-rob-davies/ |title=The Verreaux's Eagle- An Interview with Dr Rob Davies |author=Munir Virani |date=13 August 2010 |publisher=African Raptors |access-date=12 August 2013}}</ref> The mean prey mass of crowned eagles in [[southern Africa]] also appears to be similar to that of martial eagles but in [[west Africa]] (i.e. [[Ivory Coast]]) it was considerably heavier at {{convert|5.67|kg|lb|abbr=on}} (which may well be the highest mean prey mass for any of the world's raptors).<ref name="Tarboton" /><ref>{{cite journal |author=Shultz S., Noë R., McGraw W. S., Dunbar R. I. M. |year=2004 |title=A community-level evaluation of the impact of prey behavioural and ecological characteristics on predator diet composition |journal=Proceedings of the Royal Society of London B: Biological Sciences |volume=271 |issue=1540 |pages=725–732 |doi=10.1098/rspb.2003.2626 |pmid=15209106 |pmc=1691645}}</ref> Elsewhere, mean prey masses for the larger booted eagles appears to be considerably smaller than in the larger African species, i.e. single studies for the [[Spanish imperial eagle]] (''Aquila adalberti'') and [[wedge-tailed eagle]]s showed means of {{convert|0.45|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and {{convert|1.3|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, respectively, while a large number of extensive dietary studies for the golden eagle show its global mean prey mass is around {{convert|1.61|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Watson" /><ref>Sánchez, R., Margalida, A., González, L.M., & Oria, J. (2008). ''Biases in diet sampling methods in the Spanish Imperial Eagle Aquila adalberti''. Ornis Fennica, 85(3), 82-89.</ref><ref>Olsen, J., Judge, D., Fuentes, E., Rose, A. B., & Debus, S.J.S. (2010). ''Diets of wedge-tailed eagles (''Aquila audax'') and little eagles (''Hieraaetus morphnoides'') breeding near Canberra, Australia''. Journal of Raptor Research, 44(1), 50-61.</ref>
